title: Research Bits: June 23
gist: Three academic research advances cover: (1) a proposed high-NA EUV redesign at OIST that could cut lithography machine costs by 75%; (2) KAIST's embedded manifold microchannel liquid cooling achieving COP of 106,000 under 2,000 W/cm² heat loads with one-tenth the pump power of conventional cooling; and (3) RMIT/Zhejiang University's in-sensor light wavelength analysis using laser-inscribed spiral structures integrated directly with commercial image sensors.

insights.claim: An OIST researcher proposes redesigning high-NA EUV illumination systems and projectors using simpler collector mirrors with multi-reflection aberration cancellation, targeting 2–3 nm feature resolution at approximately one-quarter the cost of current state-of-the-art high-NA EUV machines that cost hundreds of millions of euros each.
insights.speaker: Jesse Allen
entities: Okinawa Institute of Science and Technology
insights.so_what: If validated, a 75% reduction in high-NA EUV tool cost would be a major disruption to ASML's dominance at the extreme end of lithography; this is academic research at early stage — require peer replication before treating as credible competitive threat.
insights.quote: My new design should achieve fine, 2-3 nm scale details, in a much cheaper way compared to today's state-of-the-art machines... We should be able to create machines that are a quarter of the cost of those currently on the market.

themes.theme: Embedded Liquid Cooling for High-Power Chips

insights.claim: KAIST researchers embedded manifold microchannel (MMC) liquid-cooling structures directly inside silicon chips, maintaining chip temperature below 100°C under extreme heat loads exceeding 2,000 W/cm² using room-temperature water — the cooling structure achieved a coefficient of performance (COP) of 106,000.
insights.speaker: Jesse Allen
entities: Korea Advanced Institute of Science and Technology
insights.so_what: AI accelerator thermal limits are increasingly constraining chip performance density; in-silicon liquid cooling at COP 106,000 — using one-tenth the pump power of conventional approaches — could be a path to sustaining power density scaling for next-generation AI chips.
insights.quote: using room-temperature water, was able to maintain chip temperature below 100°C even under extreme heat-generation conditions exceeding 2,000 watts per square centimeter

insights.claim: KAIST's MMC cooling system was fabricated using a low-temperature process below 350°C compatible with conventional semiconductor manufacturing, meaning it could in principle be integrated into standard chip production flows rather than requiring exotic backend processes.
insights.speaker: Jesse Allen
insights.so_what: Process compatibility with standard semiconductor fabs is the key commercialization gate for embedded cooling; this detail matters more than thermal performance alone for assessing whether this technology reaches production.
insights.quote: The device was fabricated using a low-temperature process below 350°C that is compatible with conventional semiconductor manufacturing.

insights.claim: Researchers from Zhejiang University and RMIT University used ultrafast laser pulses to create microscopic spiral structures inside transparent materials that sort light by wavelength before it reaches the sensor, enabling spectral imaging capability to be built directly into standard image sensor hardware rather than added in post-processing software.
insights.speaker: Jesse Allen
insights.so_what: Embedding spectral analysis at the sensor level could dramatically reduce system cost for hyperspectral imaging applications (food inspection, medical diagnostics, autonomous vehicles); watch for commercialization pathway through RMIT's CAN center.
insights.quote: This is not about adding more image processing after the fact. It introduces a new physical component that separates light at a very small scale, close to the sensor itself.
